title: Act III
html:
Act III

The scene is Florence‚Äôs bedroom the same night. About two hours have elapsed. When the curtain rises Florence is lying face downwards on the bed; she is dressed in a very beautiful but slightly exotic negligee.

Helen is standing by the window, fully dressed; she is holding the curtain aside, and a bar of moonlight comes in to mingle with the amber of the dressing-table lights. Florence is obviously extremely hysterical.

Helen Florence, what is the use of going on like that?
Florence I wish I were dead!
Helen It‚Äôs so cowardly to give way utterlyÔªø‚Äîas you‚Äôre doing.
Florence I don‚Äôt careÔªø‚ÄîI don‚Äôt care!
Helen If you don‚Äôt face things in this world, they only hit you much harder in the end.
Florence He loved meÔªø‚Äîhe adored me!
Helen Never! He hadn‚Äôt got it in him.
Florence After all I‚Äôve done for him, to go toÔªø‚Äîto Bunty!
Helen

Leaving the window.

If it hadn‚Äôt been Bunty it would have been someone elseÔªø‚Äîdon‚Äôt you see how inevitable it was?
Florence How dared they!Ôªø‚ÄîHere!Ôªø‚ÄîIn this house!
Helen That‚Äôs a little thing; it doesn‚Äôt matter at all.
Florence It doesÔªø‚Äîit doesÔªø‚Äî
Helen Florence, sit up and pull yourself together.
Florence

Sitting up slowly.

I think I‚Äôm going mad.
Helen Not a bit of it; you‚Äôre just thoroughly hysterical.
Florence Give me some water.
Helen goes to the bathroom and returns with a glass of water.
Florence

Taking it.

What time is it?
Helen

Looking at her watch.

Ten past one.
Florence Don‚Äôt go to London by the early train, Helen; stay and come up with me in the car.
Helen Very well.
Florence Thank God, you were here!
Helen I wish I‚Äôd known what was happening; I might have done something.
Florence What can I do to get him back?
Helen Don‚Äôt be silly.
Florence What can I doÔªø‚Äîwhat can I do?Ôªø‚Äî
Helen Do you mean to say you‚Äôd take him back after tonight?
Florence No, never. Not if he crawled to meÔªø‚ÄîneverÔªø‚Äî
Helen Well, then, make up your mind definitely never to see him again whatever happens.
Florence YesÔªø‚ÄîI will.
Helen Why don‚Äôt you go to bed now?
Florence I couldn‚Äôt sleep.
Helen Put it all out of your mindÔªø‚Äîmake an effort.
Florence I can‚ÄôtÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôm too unhappy.
Helen Think of Nicky.
Florence Nicky‚Äôs young.
Helen That doesn‚Äôt make it any better for him.
Florence He‚Äôll get over it in the long run.
Helen The long run never counts at the moment.
Florence He wasn‚Äôt in loveÔªø‚Äîreally?
Helen As much as either you or he are capable of it.
Florence He‚Äôs well rid of her. She‚Äôd never have appreciated him properlyÔªø‚Äîshe hasn‚Äôt the intelligence.
Helen I don‚Äôt agree with you thereÔªø‚Äîshe‚Äôs got intelligence right enough.
Florence Treacherous little beast!
Helen Yes, but farseeing.
Florence Are you standing up for her? Do you think it was right of her to get Tom away from me?
Helen Yes, quite right.
Florence Helen!
Helen To do her justice, she didn‚Äôt deliberately set herself out to get him away from you at all. She discovered that in spite of the somewhat decadent years Tom was still her type, and likely to remain so. So with common sense she decided to shelve Nicky forthwith and go for him.
Florence Her type indeed!
Helen Yes, she‚Äôd have been quite a nice girl really if she‚Äôd been left alone and not allowed to go to Paris and get into the wrong set.
Florence You are extraordinary, Helen. Do you realize that you‚Äôre making excuses for the girl who‚Äôs betrayed your best friend?
Helen Don‚Äôt be so utterly absurd. I‚Äôm not making excuses, and, anyhow, she hasn‚Äôt betrayed you. She hardly knows you, in the first place, and she‚Äôs just followed her instincts regardless of anyone else‚Äôs feelingsÔªø‚Äîas you‚Äôve done thousands of times.
Florence HelenÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre being horrible to me!
Helen I‚Äôm not, I‚Äôm trying to make you see! You‚Äôre battering your head against silly cast-iron delusions, and I want to dislodge them.
Florence Helen, I‚Äôm so unhappyÔªø‚Äîso desperately unhappy.
Helen Yes, but not because you‚Äôve lost Tom; it‚Äôs something far deeper than that.
Florence What then?
Helen You‚Äôre on the wrong tack, and have been for years.
Florence I don‚Äôt understand.
Helen You won‚Äôt understand!
Florence gets off the bed and goes over to the dressing-table. She sits and stares at herself in the glass for a moment without speaking.
Florence My eyes are sore. She powders her face and sprays a little scent on her hair. It‚Äôs so lovely thisÔªø‚Äîand so refreshing.
Helen I think I‚Äôll go to bed now.
Florence No, wait a little longer with meÔªø‚Äîplease, HelenÔªø‚Äîjust a few minutes.
Helen It‚Äôs so hot in here.
Florence Open the window then.
Helen All right.
She goes to the window and opens it. Florence takes a cigarette out of a box and then shakes a scent-bottle and rubs the cigarette lightly with the stopper.
Florence Do you ever do this? It‚Äôs divine.
Helen What a wonderfully clear night. You can see the hills right across the valleyÔªø‚Äîthe moon‚Äôs quite strong.
Florence goes to the window and stands next to Helen, looking outÔªø‚Äîshe is puffing her cigarette.
Florence I chose this room in the first place because the view was so lovely.
Helen Do you ever look at it?
Florence

Listlessly.

Of course I do, often!
Helen It‚Äôs been raining. I wish you‚Äôd throw away that cigaretteÔªø‚Äîit spoils the freshness.
Florence

Turning away.

It‚Äôs soothing meÔªø‚Äîcalming my nerves.
Helen I do wish I could help youÔªø‚Äîreally!
Florence You are helping me, darlingÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre being an angel.
Helen

Suddenly angry.

Don‚Äôt talk so emptily, Florence; I‚Äôm worth more than that.
Florence I don‚Äôt know what you mean.
Helen It sickens me to see you getting back so soon.
Florence Getting back?
Helen Yes, to your usual worthless attitude of mind.
Florence Helen!
Helen A little while ago you were really suffering for once, and in a way I was glad because it showed you were capable of a genuine emotion. Now you‚Äôre glossing it overÔªø‚Äîswarming it down with your returning vanity; soon you won‚Äôt be unhappy any moreÔªø‚Äîjust vindictive.
Florence Don‚Äôt go on at me like thatÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôm too wretched.
Helen

Going to her.

Florence dear, forgive me, but it‚Äôs trueÔªø‚Äîand I don‚Äôt want it to be.
The door opens and Nicky enters. He is in dressing-gown and pyjamas. His face looks strained and white.
Florence Nicky!
Nicky Helen, I want to talk to mother, please.
Helen All right, Nicky.
Florence What is it?
Nicky I couldn‚Äôt sleep.
Helen Florence dearÔªø‚Äîgood night.
Florence NoÔªø‚Äîno, HelenÔªø‚Äîdon‚Äôt go yetÔªø‚Äî
Helen I must.
Florence HelenÔªø‚Äîstay with me.
Nicky Please go.
Helen I can‚Äôt stay, FlorenceÔªø‚Äîit‚Äôs quite impossible.
She goes out.
Florence I don‚Äôt know what you meanÔªø‚Äîby coming here and ordering Helen out of my room.
Nicky I‚Äôm sorry, mother. I felt I had to talk to you alone.
Florence At this hour of the night? You‚Äôre mad!
Nicky No, I‚Äôm not; I think I‚Äôm probably more unhappy than I‚Äôve ever been in my life.
Florence You‚Äôre youngÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôll get over it.
Nicky I hope so.
Florence I knew the first moment I saw herÔªø‚Äîwhat sort of a girl she was.
Nicky Oh, mother!
Florence It‚Äôs true. I had an instinct about her.
Nicky It‚Äôs all been rather a shock, you knowÔªø‚Äî
Florence

Becoming motherly.

Yes, dearÔªø‚ÄîI knowÔªø‚ÄîI knowÔªø‚Äîbut you mustn‚Äôt be miserable about her; she isn‚Äôt worth it. She goes to kiss him.
Nicky

Gently pushing her away.

Don‚Äôt, mother!
Florence Listen, Nicky. Go back to bed nowÔªø‚Äîthere‚Äôs a dear; my head‚Äôs splitting.
Nicky I can‚Äôt yet.
Florence Take some aspirin; that‚Äôll calm your nerves.
Nicky I‚Äôm afraid I‚Äôm a little beyond aspirin.
Florence I don‚Äôt want you to think I don‚Äôt sympathize with you, darlingÔªø‚Äîmy heart aches for youÔªø‚ÄîI know so well what you‚Äôre going through.
Nicky Do you?
Florence It‚Äôs agonyÔªø‚Äîabsolute agonyÔªø‚Äîbut, you seeÔªø‚Äîit will wear offÔªø‚Äîit always does in time. Nicky doesn‚Äôt answer. Nicky, please go now!
Nicky I want to talk to you.
Florence TomorrowÔªø‚Äîwe‚Äôll talk tomorrow.
Nicky No, nowÔªø‚Äînow!
Florence You‚Äôre inconsiderate and cruelÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôve told you my head‚Äôs bursting.
Nicky I want to sympathize with you, tooÔªø‚Äîand try to understand everythingÔªø‚Äîas well as I canÔªø‚Äî
Florence Understand everything?
Nicky Yes, please.
Florence I don‚Äôt know what you meanÔªø‚Äî
Nicky Will you tell me thingsÔªø‚Äîas though I were somebody quite different?
Florence What kind of things?
Nicky Things about youÔªø‚Äîyour life.
Florence Really, NickyÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre ridiculousÔªø‚Äîasking me to tell you stories at this hour!
Nicky

With dead vehemence.

MotherÔªø‚Äîsit down quietly. I‚Äôm not going out of this room until I‚Äôve got everything straight in my mind.
Florence

Sinking downÔªø‚Äîalmost hypnotized.

NickyÔªø‚ÄîpleaseÔªø‚ÄîIÔªø‚Äî
Nicky Tom Veryan has been your lover, hasn‚Äôt he?
Florence

Almost shrieking.

NickyÔªø‚Äîhow dare you!
Nicky Keep calmÔªø‚Äîit‚Äôs our only chanceÔªø‚Äîkeep calm.
Florence

Bursting into tears.

How dare you speak to me like thatÔªø‚Äîsuggest such a thing! IÔªø‚Äî
Nicky It‚Äôs true, isn‚Äôt it?
Florence Go awayÔªø‚Äîgo away!
Nicky It‚Äôs true, isn‚Äôt it?
Florence NoÔªø‚Äîno!
Nicky It‚Äôs true, isn‚Äôt it?
Florence NoÔªø‚ÄîI tell youÔªø‚ÄînoÔªø‚ÄînoÔªø‚Äîno!
Nicky You‚Äôre lying to me, mother. What‚Äôs the use of that?
Florence You‚Äôre madÔªø‚ÄîmadÔªø‚Äî
Nicky Does father know?
Florence Go away!
Nicky Does father know?
Florence Your father knows nothingÔªø‚Äîhe doesn‚Äôt understand me any more than you do.
Nicky Then it‚Äôs between us alone.
Florence I tell you I don‚Äôt know what you‚Äôre talking about.
Nicky MotherÔªø‚Äîdon‚Äôt go on like that; it‚Äôs useless. We‚Äôve arrived at a crisis; wherever we goÔªø‚Äîwhatever we do we can‚Äôt escape from it. I know we‚Äôre neither of us very strong-minded or capable, and we haven‚Äôt much hope of coming through successfullyÔªø‚Äîbut let‚Äôs try. It‚Äôs no good pretending any moreÔªø‚Äîour lives are built up of pretenses all the time. For yearsÔªø‚Äîever since I began to think at all, I‚Äôve been bolstering up my illusions about you. People have made remarks, not realizing that I was your son, and I‚Äôve pretended that they were inspired by cattiness and jealousy. I‚Äôve noticed thingsÔªø‚Äîtrivial incriminating little incidents, and I‚Äôve brushed them aside and not thought any more about them because you were my motherÔªø‚Äîclever and beautiful and successfulÔªø‚Äîand naturally people would slander you because you were so beautifulÔªø‚Äîand now I knowÔªø‚Äîthey were right!
Florence NickyÔªø‚ÄîI implore youÔªø‚Äîgo away nowÔªø‚Äîleave me alone.
Nicky No, I can‚Äôt.
Florence You‚Äôre cruelÔªø‚Äîcruel to torment meÔªø‚Äî
Nicky I don‚Äôt want to be cruelÔªø‚Äî
Florence Go to bed then, and we‚Äôll talk everything over quietly another time.
Nicky It is true about Tom Veryan, isn‚Äôt it?
Florence No. NoÔªø‚Äî
Nicky We‚Äôre on awfully dangerous ground. I‚Äôm straining every nerve to keep myself under control. If you lie to me and try to evade me any moreÔªø‚ÄîI won‚Äôt be answerable for what might happen.
Florence

Dropping her voiceÔªø‚Äîterrified.

What do you mean?
Nicky I don‚Äôt knowÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôm frightened.
Florence NickyÔªø‚Äîdarling NickyÔªø‚ÄîIÔªø‚Äî
She approaches him.
Nicky Don‚Äôt touch me, please.
Florence Have a little pity for me.
Nicky Was Tom Veryan your lover?
Florence

In a whisper.

Yes.
Nicky I want to understand whyÔªø‚Äî
Florence He loved me.
Nicky But youÔªø‚Äîdid you love him?
Florence Yes.
Nicky It was something you couldn‚Äôt help, wasn‚Äôt itÔªø‚Äîsomething that‚Äôs always been the same in you since you were quite, quite young?Ôªø‚Äî
Florence Yes, NickyÔªø‚ÄîyesÔªø‚Äî
Nicky And there have been others, too, haven‚Äôt there?
Florence

With her face in her hands.

I won‚Äôt be cross-questioned any moreÔªø‚ÄîI won‚ÄôtÔªø‚ÄîI won‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äî
Nicky I wish you‚Äôd understand I‚Äôm not blaming youÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôm trying to help youÔªø‚Äîto help us bothÔªø‚Äî
Florence What good can all this possibly do?
Nicky Clear things up, of course. I can‚Äôt go on any more half knowingÔªø‚Äî
Florence Why should that side of my life be any concern of yours?
Nicky But, mother!
Florence I‚Äôm different from other womenÔªø‚Äîcompletely differentÔªø‚Äîand you expect me to be the same. Why can‚Äôt you realize that with a temperament like mine it‚Äôs impossible to live an ordinary humdrum life. You‚Äôre not a boy any longerÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre a manÔªø‚ÄîandÔªø‚Äî
Nicky I‚Äôm nothingÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôve grown up all wrong.
Florence It‚Äôs not my fault.
Nicky Of course it‚Äôs your fault, motherÔªø‚Äîwho else‚Äôs fault could it be?
Florence Your friendsÔªø‚Äîthe people you mix withÔªø‚Äî
Nicky It wouldn‚Äôt matter who I mixed with if only I had a background.
Florence You‚Äôve got as much money as you wantÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôve got your homeÔªø‚Äî
Nicky

Bitterly.

Home! That‚Äôs almost funnyÔªø‚Äîthere‚Äôs no peace anywhereÔªø‚Äînothing but the ceaseless din of trying to be amusedÔªø‚Äî
Florence David never complains.
Nicky I don‚Äôt suppose you‚Äôve looked at father during the last few yearsÔªø‚Äîor you wouldn‚Äôt say that.
Florence He‚Äôs perfectly happy because he‚Äôs sensibleÔªø‚Äîhe lives his own life and doesn‚Äôt try to interfere with mine.
Nicky It must be your vanity that makes you so dreadfully blindÔªø‚Äîand foolish.
Florence Understand once and for all, I won‚Äôt be spoken to like thisÔªø‚Äî
Nicky You‚Äôve had other lovers besides Tom VeryanÔªø‚Äîhaven‚Äôt you?
Florence Yes, I haveÔªø‚ÄîI have. Now then!
Nicky Well, anyhowÔªø‚Äîthat‚Äôs the truthÔªø‚Äîat lastÔªø‚Äî
He rises, turns his back on her and stands looking out of the window.
Florence

After a pauseÔªø‚Äîgoing to him.

NickyÔªø‚Äîdon‚Äôt be angryÔªø‚Äîplease don‚Äôt be angry with me.
Nicky I‚Äôm not angry a bit. I realize that I‚Äôm living in a world where things like this happenÔªø‚Äîand they‚Äôve got to be faced and given the right value. If only I‚Äôd had the courage to realize everything beforeÔªø‚Äîit wouldn‚Äôt be so bad now. It‚Äôs the sudden shock that‚Äôs thrown the whole thing out of focus for meÔªø‚Äîbut I mean to get it right. Please help me!
Florence

Dully.

I don‚Äôt know what to do.
Nicky It‚Äôs your life, and you‚Äôve lived it as you‚Äôve wanted to live itÔªø‚Äîthat‚Äôs fairÔªø‚Äî
Florence YesÔªø‚Äîyes.
Nicky You‚Äôve wanted love alwaysÔªø‚Äîpassionate love, because you were made like that. It‚Äôs not your faultÔªø‚Äîit‚Äôs the fault of circumstances and civilization; civilization makes rottenness so much easier. We‚Äôre utterly rottenÔªø‚Äîboth of usÔªø‚Äî
Florence NickyÔªø‚Äîdon‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äîdon‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äî
Nicky How can we help ourselves? We swirl about in a vortex of beastliness. This is a chanceÔªø‚Äîdon‚Äôt you seeÔªø‚Äîto realize the truthÔªø‚Äîour only chance.
Florence Oh, Nicky, do stopÔªø‚Äîgo away!
Nicky Don‚Äôt keep on telling me to stop when our only hope is to hammer it out.
Florence You‚Äôre overwrought. It isn‚Äôt as bad as you think.
Nicky Isn‚Äôt it?
Florence No, no. Of course it isn‚Äôt. Tomorrow morning you‚Äôll see things quite differently.
Nicky You haven‚Äôt understood.
Florence Yes, I haveÔªø‚ÄîI have.
Nicky You haven‚Äôt understood. Oh, my God, you haven‚Äôt understood! You‚Äôre building up silly defenses in your mind. I‚Äôm overwrought. Tomorrow morning I shall see things quite differently. That‚Äôs trueÔªø‚Äîthat‚Äôs the tragedy of it, and you won‚Äôt see. Tomorrow morning I shall see things quite differently. All this will seem unrealÔªø‚Äîa nightmareÔªø‚Äîthe machinery of our lives will go on again and gloss over the truth as it always doesÔªø‚Äîand our chance will be gone forever.
Florence ChanceÔªø‚Äîchance? What are you talking aboutÔªø‚Äîwhat chance?
Nicky I must make you see, somehow.
Florence You‚Äôre driving me mad.
Nicky Have patience with meÔªø‚ÄîpleaseÔªø‚ÄîpleaseÔªø‚Äî
Florence

Wildly.

How can I have patience with you? You exaggerate everything.
Nicky No I don‚ÄôtÔªø‚ÄîI wish I did.
Florence ListenÔªø‚Äîlet me explain something to you.
Nicky Very wellÔªø‚Äîgo on.
Florence You‚Äôre setting yourself up in judgment on meÔªø‚Äîyour own mother.
Nicky No, I‚Äôm not.
Florence You areÔªø‚Äîyou are. Let me speak. You don‚Äôt understand my temperament in the leastÔªø‚Äînobody doesÔªø‚ÄîIÔªø‚Äî
Nicky You‚Äôre deceiving yourselfÔªø‚Äîyour temperament‚Äôs no different from thousands of other women, but you‚Äôve been weak and selfish and given way all along the lineÔªø‚Äî
Florence Let me speak, I tell you!Ôªø‚Äî
Nicky What‚Äôs the use? You‚Äôre still pretendingÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre building up barriers between us instead of helping me to break them down.
Florence What are you accusing me of having done?
Nicky Can‚Äôt you see yet?
Florence No, I can‚Äôt. If you‚Äôre preaching morality, you‚Äôve no right to. That‚Äôs my affairÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôve never done any harm to anyone.
Nicky Look at me.
Florence WhyÔªø‚Äîwhat do you mean?
Nicky You‚Äôve given me nothing all my lifeÔªø‚Äînothing that counts.
Florence Now you‚Äôre pitying yourself.
Nicky Yes, with every reason.
Florence You‚Äôre neurotic and ridiculous. Just because Bunty broke off your engagement you come and say wicked, cruel things to meÔªø‚Äî
Nicky You forget what I‚Äôve seen tonight, mother.
Florence I don‚Äôt care what you‚Äôve seen.
Nicky I‚Äôve seen you make a vulgar, disgusting scene in your own house, and on top of that humiliate yourself before a boy half your age. The misery of losing Bunty faded away when that happenedÔªø‚Äîeverything is comparative, after all.
Florence I didn‚Äôt humiliate myselfÔªø‚Äî
Nicky You ran after him up the stairs because your vanity wouldn‚Äôt let you lose him. It isn‚Äôt that you love himÔªø‚Äîthat would be easierÔªø‚Äîyou never love anyone, you only love them loving youÔªø‚Äîall your so-called passion and temperament is falseÔªø‚Äîyour whole existence had degenerated into an endless empty craving for admiration and flatteryÔªø‚Äîand then you say you‚Äôve done no harm to anybody. Father used to be a clever man, with a strong will and a capacity for enjoying everythingÔªø‚ÄîI can remember him like thatÔªø‚Äîand now he‚Äôs nothingÔªø‚Äîa complete nonentity because his spirit‚Äôs crushed. How could it be otherwise? You‚Äôve let him down consistently for yearsÔªø‚Äîand God knows I‚Äôm nothing for him to look forward toÔªø‚Äîbut I might have been if it hadn‚Äôt been for youÔªø‚Äî
Florence Don‚Äôt talk like that. Don‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äîdon‚Äôt. It can‚Äôt be such a crime being lovedÔªø‚Äîit can‚Äôt be such a crime being happyÔªø‚Äî
Nicky You‚Äôre not happyÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre never happyÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre fightingÔªø‚Äîfighting all the time to keep your youth and your looksÔªø‚Äîbecause you can‚Äôt bear the thought of living without themÔªø‚Äîas though they mattered in the end.
Florence

Hysterically.

What does anything matterÔªø‚Äîever?
Nicky That‚Äôs what I‚Äôm trying to find out.
Florence I‚Äôm still young insideÔªø‚ÄîI‚Äôm still beautiful. Why shouldn‚Äôt I live my life as I choose?
Nicky You‚Äôre not young or beautiful; I‚Äôm seeing for the first time how old you are. It‚Äôs horribleÔªø‚Äîyour silly fair hairÔªø‚Äîand your face all plastered and paintedÔªø‚Äî
Florence NickyÔªø‚ÄîNickyÔªø‚ÄîstopÔªø‚ÄîstopÔªø‚Äîstop!
She flings herself face downwards on the bed. Nicky goes over to her.
Nicky Mother!
Florence Go awayÔªø‚Äîgo awayÔªø‚ÄîI hate youÔªø‚Äîgo awayÔªø‚Äî
Nicky MotherÔªø‚Äîsit upÔªø‚Äî
Florence

Pulling herself together.

Go out of my roomÔªø‚Äî
Nicky MotherÔªø‚Äî
Florence I don‚Äôt ever want to see you againÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôre insaneÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôve said wicked, wicked things to meÔªø‚Äîyou‚Äôve talked to me as though I were a woman off the streets. I can‚Äôt bear any moreÔªø‚ÄîI can‚Äôt bear any more!
Nicky I have a slight confession to makeÔªø‚Äî
Florence Confession?
Nicky Yes.
Florence Go awayÔªø‚Äîgo awayÔªø‚Äî
Nicky

Taking a small gold box from his pocket.

LookÔªø‚Äî
Florence What do you meanÔªø‚Äîwhat is itÔªø‚Äî?
Nicky Don‚Äôt you know?
Florence takes the box with trembling fingers and opens it. She stares at it for a moment. When she speaks again her voice is quite dead.
Florence Nicky, it isn‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äîyou haven‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äî?
Nicky Why do you look so shocked?
Florence

Dully.

Oh, my God!
Nicky

What does it matter?

Florence suddenly rises and hurls the box out of the window.

That doesn‚Äôt make it any better.
